fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to wrap up that cross-platform desktop notification routing library we've been planning. The basic idea is that app developers should only have to write one options object and the library handles all the messy platform differences under the hood — no more hand-rolling shell commands or worrying about which binary to call on what machine.

A few things I want to make sure we nail: the library should know about all the notification senders we support and be able to tell you which ones exist plus confirm a default one is ready to go. Each sender has its own quirky naming conventions for things like icons and urgency, so we need that normalization layer that maps our friendly option names into whatever that specific tool actually expects. One of the senders (the network-push one) is a bit special because it needs to handle icon data differently from the others — remember how we handled binary assets in that earlier media pipeline work, same kind of idea.

Also really important: the command arguments each tool gets need to be constructed safely — users can put weird characters in their notification text and we can't let that cause problems. Windows has that extra wrinkle where you need to pick the right binary depending on the machine architecture. And the quiet/sound behavior on Windows needs to be toggleable. Finally, any request missing a message should be rejected cleanly before anything gets dispatched. Keep the code organized — no giant single files please.

trigger_keywords: which backends are supported
list of available notifiers
what senders does the library expose
registry of notification providers
how many backends
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The library exposes exactly five named backends: Growl, NotificationCenter, NotifySend, WindowsBalloon, and WindowsToaster. When queried, they must be returned in alphabetically sorted order, one per line as 'backend=<Name>', followed by a 'default_notify=available' line confirming the default sender is ready. The set is fixed and does not change based on host platform.

trigger_keywords: icon option normalization notify-send
Linux icon key mapping
appIcon shorthand linux
i shorthand for icon notify-send
how does icon get mapped on linux
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For the Linux notify-send backend, both the generic 'appIcon' key and the single-letter 'i' shorthand must collapse to the canonical key 'icon'. Additionally, the full set of single-letter shorthands expands as follows: u→urgency, e→expire-time, c→category, i→icon, h→hint. Title and message pass through unchanged.

trigger_keywords: mac icon normalization
macOS appIcon key
terminal-notifier icon alias
icon shorthand mac backend
how is icon handled for macOS
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For the macOS (terminal-notifier) backend, the generic 'icon' key and the 'i' shorthand both collapse to the canonical key 'appIcon'. Title and message pass through unchanged. This is the inverse of the Linux backend: Linux uses 'icon' as canonical, macOS uses 'appIcon' as canonical.

trigger_keywords: growl icon binary
network push icon bytes
GNTP icon as data
app-icon placeholder growl
icon materialized as bytes
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The network-push (Growl/GNTP) backend requires the icon to be loaded as raw binary bytes rather than a file path string. The placeholder '@app-icon' refers to a bundled 16-byte image asset included with the library. Whether the icon is supplied via the 'icon' key or the 'appIcon' key, it must be read into a byte buffer and exposed under the key 'icon'. The rendered output format for the test adapter is 'bytes:<length>' (e.g., 'bytes:16').

trigger_keywords: notify-send argument vector
Linux command construction
positional arguments notify-send
default title linux notifier
how are title and message passed to notify-send
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For the Linux notify-send backend, title and message are the first two positional arguments, each wrapped in double quotes. If title is absent, it defaults to 'Node Notification:'. Supported extra options (like 'icon') become '--<name>' flags followed by their quoted value. Unsupported options are silently dropped. Special shell metacharacters (double quotes, backticks, dollar signs, backslashes) in any text value must be backslash-escaped before wrapping in double quotes.

trigger_keywords: windows balloon architecture selection
notifu 32 bit vs 64 bit
executable selection by arch
x64 ia32 notifu binary
which notifu exe to use
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The Windows balloon (notifu) backend selects its executable based on CPU architecture: a 64-bit host (x64) resolves to 'notifu64.exe', and a 32-bit host (ia32) resolves to 'notifu.exe'. When no arch is specified in the request, the default assumption is x64 (notifu64.exe).

trigger_keywords: notifu argument flags
windows notifier single dash flags
notifu message title quiet default
how does notifu build its arguments
notifu default title missing
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The notifu backend uses single-dash flags: message under '-m', title under '-p', display duration under '-d', icon under '-i'. The '-q' (quiet/silent) flag is present by default. Message text is passed through literally with no quote-wrapping. A missing title defaults to 'Node Notification:'. When title is present, the order is: -m <msg> -p <title> [-d <ms>] -q. When title is absent (default substituted), order is: -m <msg> -q -p <default_title>.

trigger_keywords: notifu quiet flag toggle
disable silent mode windows
sound option removes quiet flag
quiet false notifu
how to enable sound on windows notifier
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The '-q' flag is included by default. It is removed from the argument vector in two cases: (1) if the 'sound' option is set to true, or (2) if the 'quiet' option is explicitly set to false. Boolean flags follow explicit-true semantics: a true boolean value emits the bare flag, a false boolean value omits the flag entirely.

trigger_keywords: mac args construction terminal-notifier
macOS argument vector
terminal-notifier passthrough options
unsupported keys mac backend
how does macOS notifier build command
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The macOS (terminal-notifier) backend converts every option into a '-<key>' flag followed by its double-quote-wrapped value. Unlike other backends, it does NOT silently drop unsupported/unrecognized keys — all options are passed through. Embedded double quotes inside values are backslash-escaped before wrapping.

trigger_keywords: message required validation
missing message error
validate notification request
what happens with no message
request rejected without message
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Validation must be performed before dispatch. A request that does not include a 'message' field must be rejected with the error category 'message_required'. A request that includes a message passes with 'error=none'. This rule applies uniformly across all backends. The error must be reported as a modeled result, not a crash or silent empty dispatch.

trigger_keywords: growl host port configuration
GNTP server address
network push constructor options
growl appname default
how to set host and port for growl
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The Growl/GNTP backend accepts 'host' and 'port' as constructor-level configuration (not per-message options). These are forwarded to the underlying push transport when sending. When not supplied, both remain unset (rendered as '<unset>' in test output). The application name defaults to 'Node' regardless of whether host/port are provided.

trigger_keywords: shell escaping rules
which characters are escaped
metacharacter escaping
backslash escape what characters
safe text for shell command
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For backends that wrap values in double quotes (notify-send and terminal-notifier), the following characters must be backslash-escaped before wrapping: double quotes ("), backticks (`), dollar signs ($), and backslashes (\). Single quotes do NOT need escaping. The notifu (Windows) backend passes text through literally with no escaping or quote-wrapping.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: remember how we handled binary assets in that earlier media pipeline work, same kind of idea
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The Growl/GNTP backend's icon normalization logic in feature2_4: the '@app-icon' placeholder must be resolved to a bundled 16-byte binary asset and loaded into a byte buffer, not kept as a path string. See rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature2_4_map_growl_icon_bytes.json and the icon materialization logic in the Growl backend's option mapper.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the quiet/sound behavior on Windows needs to be toggleable
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The '-q' flag on the notifu backend is present by default but must be removed when 'sound: true' or 'quiet: false' is set. See rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature4_3_notifu_quiet_toggle.json for exact expected argument vectors.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the network-push one is a bit special
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The Growl (GNTP) backend differs from path-based backends in two ways: (1) icons must be materialized as raw bytes rather than path strings, and (2) it is configured with host/port at construction time rather than per-notification. See feature2_4 and feature7 test cases.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect of the requirement. Please check the existing codebase structure and the test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the expected behavior — the test files are the ground truth for exact output formatting and edge cases.

feature1_backend_registry.json.description: Lists which notification backends the library exposes as constructable senders, and confirms a default sender (bound to the current platform) is ready to use. Output is one sorted 'backend=<Name>' line per available backend plus a 'default_notify' readiness line.

cases.expected_output:
backend=Growl
backend=NotificationCenter
backend=NotifySend
backend=WindowsBalloon
backend=WindowsToaster
default_notify=available

feature2_1_map_notifysend_icon.json.description: Normalizes a user-facing option object into the shape consumed by the Linux notify-send backend. The generic application-icon key and the 'i' icon shorthand both collapse to a canonical 'icon' key; title and message are preserved. Output is one sorted 'key=value' line per resulting option.

cases.expected_output:
icon=foobar
message=Bar
title=Foo

icon=foobar
message=Bar
title=Foo

feature2_2_map_notifysend_shorthand.json.description: Expands single-letter option shorthands into their full notify-send option names: u->urgency, e->expire-time, c->category, i->icon, h->hint. Output is one sorted 'key=value' line per resulting option.

cases.expected_output:
category=c
expire-time=b
hint=e
icon=d
urgency=a

feature2_3_map_mac_icon.json.description: Normalizes a user-facing option object into the shape consumed by the macOS notification backend. The generic 'icon' key and the 'i' icon shorthand both collapse to a canonical application-icon key ('appIcon'); title and message are preserved. Output is one sorted 'key=value' line per resulting option.

cases.expected_output:
appIcon=foobar
message=Bar
title=Foo

appIcon=foobar
message=Bar
title=Foo

feature2_4_map_growl_icon_bytes.json.description: Normalizes a user-facing option object for the network-push (GNTP) backend, which requires the icon to be loaded as binary bytes rather than a path. A '@app-icon' placeholder refers to a bundled 16-byte image asset; whether supplied via the generic 'icon' key or the application-icon key, it is read into a byte buffer and exposed under 'icon'. The rendered value 'bytes:<length>' reports that the icon was materialized as binary of that byte length.

cases.expected_output:
icon=bytes:16
message=Bar
title=Foo

icon=bytes:16
message=Bar
title=Foo

feature3_1_notifysend_args_title_default.description: Builds the command vector dispatched to the Linux notify-send backend from a notification request. Title and message become the first two positional, double-quote-wrapped arguments; a missing title falls back to the default 'Node Notification:'. Output reports the resolved program name, the argument count, and each positional argument.

cases.expected_output:
program=notify-send
argc=2
argv[0]="title"
argv[1]="body"

program=notify-send
argc=2
argv[0]="Node Notification:"
argv[1]="body"

feature3_2_notifysend_args_escape.json.description: Shows that special shell metacharacters inside the message are backslash-escaped before being wrapped in double quotes when building the notify-send command vector, preventing them from being interpreted by a shell.

cases.expected_output:
program=notify-send
argc=2
argv[0]="Node Notification:"
argv[1]="some \"me'ss\`age\`\""

feature3_3_notifysend_args_flags.json.description: Shows how extra options are appended to the notify-send command vector: a supported option such as 'icon' is emitted as a '--icon' flag followed by its quoted value, while an unsupported option (here 'tullball') is silently dropped. Output reports the program, argument count, and each argument.

cases.expected_output:
program=notify-send
argc=4
argv[0]="title"
argv[1]="body"
argv[2]=--icon
argv[3]="icon-string"

argc=4
argv[0]="title"
argv[1]="body"
argv[2]=--icon
argv[3]="icon-string"

Output reports the resolved program name plus the resulting argument vector for the same title/message request.

argc=5
argv[0]=-m
argv[1]=some "me'ss`age`"
argv[2]=-q
argv[3]=-p
argv[4]=Node Notification:

feature4_3_notifu_quiet_toggle.json.description: Controls the silent/quiet ('-q') flag on the Windows notifier. By default '-q' is present; requesting sound or explicitly disabling quiet mode removes the '-q' flag from the argument vector. Output reports the program, argument count, and each argument.

cases.expected_output:
program=notifu64.exe
argc=4
argv[0]=-m
argv[1]=body
argv[2]=-p
argv[3]=Node Notification:

program=notifu64.exe
argc=4
argv[0]=-m
argv[1]=body
argv[2]=-p
argv[3]=Node Notification:

feature4_4_notifu_flags_and_time.json.description: Maps display-duration and extra flags onto the Windows notifier vector. A 'time' value becomes a '-d <ms>' duration flag. Boolean flags follow explicit-true semantics: a true boolean ('w') is emitted as a bare '-w' flag, while a false boolean is omitted entirely. Unsupported options are dropped. Output reports the program, argument count, and each argument.

cases.expected_output:
program=notifu64.exe
argc=7
argv[0]=-m
argv[1]=body
argv[2]=-p
argv[3]=title
argv[4]=-d
argv[5]=1000
argv[6]=-q

program=notifu64.exe
argc=9
argv[0]=-d
argv[1]=1000
argv[2]=-i
argv[3]=icon
argv[4]=-m
argv[5]=body
argv[6]=-p
argv[7]=title
argv[8]=-q

program=notifu64.exe
argc=10
argv[0]=-d
argv[1]=1000
argv[2]=-w
argv[3]=-i
argv[4]=icon
argv[5]=-m
argv[6]=body
argv[7]=-p
argv[8]=title
argv[9]=-q

program=notifu64.exe
argc=5
argv[0]=-m
argv[1]=body
argv[2]=-p
argv[3]=title
argv[4]=-q

feature5_mac_args_passthrough_escape.jso.description: Builds the argument vector for the macOS notifier. Every option becomes a '-<key>' flag followed by its double-quote-wrapped value (including non-sensical/unsupported keys, which are passed through rather than rejected). Embedded double quotes inside title and message are backslash-escaped. Output reports the program, argument count, and each argument.

cases.expected_output:
program=terminal-notifier
argc=6
argv[0]=-title
argv[1]="title"
argv[2]=-message
argv[3]="body"
argv[4]=-tullball
argv[5]="notValid"

program=terminal-notifier
argc=6
argv[0]=-title
argv[1]="title \"message\""
argv[2]=-message
argv[3]="body \"message\""
argv[4]=-tullball
argv[5]="notValid"

feature6_message_required_validation.jso.description: Validates a notification request before dispatch: a request without a message is rejected with the neutral error category 'message_required' and no command is dispatched, while a request that includes a message passes validation ('error=none'). The same rule holds across backends. Output is a single 'error=<category>' line.

cases.expected_output: error=message_required
error=message_required
error=none

feature7_growl_host_port.json.description: Configures the network-push (GNTP) backend with an overridable server host and port supplied at construction time, then forwards those values to the underlying push transport when sending. When host/port are provided they appear on the transport; when omitted they remain unset. The application name defaults to 'Node'. Output reports the effective appname, host, and port the transport will use.

cases.expected_output:
appname=Node
host=foo
port=bar

appname=Node
host=<unset>
port=<unset>
